name: Observation Interface & Recoding
description: The sensorimotor boundary + inscription process that transduces a Domain Snapshot into a persistent encoded artifact.

name: Epistemic Artifact
description: Structured object that encodes claims, constraints, or distributions over reality; the primary carrier of semantic content.

specific.coreAssumptions: Artifacts are structured objects that encode claims

name: Interpretation Layer
description: Interpretation function that assigns meaning to an epistemic artifact.

specific.coreAssumptions: Meaning is assigned via mapping from artifact to domain

name: Epistemic Tool
description: Formal or procedural mechanism used to construct, transform, or validate epistemic artifacts.

specific.transformationRule: Varies by tool

name: Epistemic Agent
description: Entity that performs epistemic operations by applying tools to artifacts.

name: Epistemic Process (Activity)
description: Ordered sequence of tool applications over time; defines the dynamics of knowledge construction.

name: Epistemic Standard
description: Normative criteria used to evaluate validity, correctness, or acceptability of artifacts and processes.

name: Encoding Substrate
description: Encoding format or substrate in which artifacts are expressed; constrains manipulation and interpretation.

name: Epistemic Domain (Segment of Reality)
description: Segment of reality that the epistemic practice targets or models.

name: Epistemic Constraint
description: Limitation that bounds what can be known or inferred within the system.

name: Epistemic Infrastructure
description: Supporting environment that enables storage, computation, measurement, and communication of artifacts.

name: Meta‑Epistemic Layer
description: Material, energetic, social, and institutional environment enabling artifact handling over time.

name: Epistemic Feedback
description: Signal from reality (or another artifact) that resists or confirms prior predictions, driving learning.

name: Epistemic Act
description: Primitive, non‑decomposable operation performed by an agent.

name: CMOS Sensor + ADC + JPEG pipeline
description: Transduces light → digital image, compresses and stores as JPEG.

name: Philosophia Naturalis

name: Ontic Map
description: A collection of ontic maps of different domains of reality.

name: Concept (Category)
description: A structured, often implicit epistemic artifact that encodes criteria for recognition, classification, and inferential use of a class of entities or phenomena. It is not reducible to a linguistic definition but can be partially expressed through definitions, prototypes, or formal constraints.

name: Simulation
description: A generalized epistemic process that constructs executable representations of reality to generate predictive, explanatory, or exploratory state trajectories under specified assumptions and constraints.
longDescription: Simulation is a broad epistemic process through which agents construct, execute, and iteratively refine formalized representations of real or hypothetical systems in order to model their dynamics across time. It transforms concrete epistemic artifacts (initial states, parameters, governing equations) into predictive or exploratory trajectories through algorithmic operators and computational infrastructures. Simulation serves as one of the principal mechanisms for epistemic exploration when direct experimentation is costly, impossible, or unsafe. It encompasses deterministic, stochastic, mechanistic, agent-based, systems-dynamic, and numerical frameworks. Within epistemic systems, simulation links frameworks, operators, standards, constraints, and feedback loops into executable knowledge production pipelines.

historicalContext.summary: Simulation emerged from mathematical physics and military systems analysis, later expanding into engineering, computational science, economics, and machine learning as computational infrastructure advanced.

chronology.event: Early analog and Monte Carlo simulations
Digital computational simulation expansion
Large-scale multi-domain simulation ecosystems

specific.processType: Generalized computational and formal modeling process
specific.validationMethod: Empirical comparison, convergence testing, robustness analysis, falsification

relationships.description: Uses formalized artifacts as executable state representations.
Depends on operators to execute transformations over state representations.
Conforms to predictive adequacy, reproducibility, and robustness standards.
Bounded by computational complexity, uncertainty, and model incompleteness.
Simulation discrepancies inform iterative refinement.

categoryAmbiguity.primaryJustification: Primarily an epistemic process because simulation is an ordered sequence of epistemic acts generating knowledge through execution over time.

alternativeCategories.justification: Simulation can function as a broad methodological framework across disciplines.
Simulation is often used strategically for uncertainty reduction and planning.

metadata.sourceReference: General epistemic systems ontology of simulation

relationships.description: This artifact is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Artifact.

relationships.description: This standard is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Standard.

name: Abduction
description: Abduction is an epistemic process aimed at inferring and ranking candidate explanations for a given set of observations, based on how likely those observations would be under each hypothesis in a space of possible worlds.

relationships.description: This process is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Process.

name: Causality (as theoretical framework)
description: The theoretical framework and principles that define cause-and-effect relationships, often formalized through models. Provides abstract conceptual system for reasoning about influence, intervention, and explanation.

historicalContext.summary: Causality as a philosophical concept dates back to Plato and Aristotle. Modern causal frameworks emerged from Hume's regularity theory, later extended by counterfactual, probabilistic, and interventionist approaches. Since the 1990s, formal graphical and structural models (Pearl, Rubin) have dominated.

chronology.event: Aristotle formulates the Four Causes
Hume publishes 'A Treatise of Human Nature', regularity theory of causation
Lewis publishes 'Causation', counterfactual theory
Pearl introduces causal diagrams and do-calculus

specific.coreAssumptions: Causality involves counterfactual dependence, intervention, and graphical structure
specific.integrationScope: Allows reasoning about cause-effect in science

relationships.description: This framework is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Framework.
This framework formalizes the causal model blueprint.

name: Causal Model (generic representation scheme)
description: A representation of the relationships between variables in a system, aiming to understand how changes in one variable can influence or cause changes in other variables.

historicalContext.summary: Generic causal models have been used in science since at least the 20th century, with path analysis (Wright) as an early precursor. The modern notion of a formal causal model crystallized with structural equation modeling and graphical approaches.

chronology.event: Wright introduces path analysis
Structural equation models become widespread in social sciences
Pearl's 'Causality' book formalizes causal models as DAGs + structural equations

relationships.description: This blueprint is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Blueprint.

name: Structural Causal Model (SCM)
description: Uses directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) and structural equations to represent and compute counterfactuals. A specific formalism within causal modeling.

historicalContext.summary: SCM was formalized by Judea Pearl in the 1990s, building on earlier work on graphical models, structural equations, and causal Bayesian networks. It became the dominant framework for counterfactual and interventionist reasoning.

chronology.event: Pearl introduces SCM in 'Causal Diagrams for Empirical Research'
Publication of 'Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference'
Second edition of 'Causality' includes extensive SCM developments

relationships.description: This blueprint is a subtype of the generic causal model blueprint.

name: Do-calculus
description: Algebraic rules for reasoning about interventions and identifying causal effects from observational data.

historicalContext.summary: Do-calculus was developed by Judea Pearl in the mid‑1990s as a set of three rules to derive causal effects from graphical models. It provides the mathematical foundation for identifying interventions without running experiments.

chronology.event: Pearl introduces do-calculus in 'Causal Diagrams for Empirical Research'
Do-calculus presented systematically in 'Causality'
Shpitser and Pearl prove completeness of do-calculus

specific.transformationRule: three rules for manipulating conditional probabilities under interventions

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.
Do-calculus acts on causal models to identify interventions.

name: Counterfactual Reasoning
description: Reasoning about hypothetical outcomes under alternative scenarios; defining causation in terms of difference-making.

historicalContext.summary: Counterfactual reasoning has roots in Hume and was formalized by Lewis (1973). Pearl integrated it with SCM, operationalizing counterfactuals through structural equations. It is now central to causal inference and AI explainability.

chronology.event: Hume discusses counterfactuals in 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding'
Lewis publishes 'Causation' using possible-world semantics
Pearl incorporates counterfactuals into SCM

specific.transformationRule: computes outcomes under hypothetical interventions

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.

name: Potential Outcomes Framework (Rubin Model)
description: Represents causation through contrasts between potential outcomes (Y₁, Y₀) under different treatments.

historicalContext.summary: The potential outcomes framework was developed by Donald Rubin in the 1970s (building on Neyman's earlier work). It formalizes causal effects as comparisons of outcomes under treatment and control, and underpins modern causal inference in statistics and econometrics.

chronology.event: Neyman introduces potential outcomes for randomized experiments
Rubin publishes 'Estimating Causal Effects of Treatments'
Rubin formalizes the 'Rubin Causal Model'
Imbens and Angrist link potential outcomes to instrumental variables

specific.coreAssumptions: Stable unit treatment value assumption (SUTVA), no interference

relationships.description: This framework is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Framework.

name: Causal Bayesian Network
description: Probabilistic graphical models that incorporate interventions as variable manipulations.

historicalContext.summary: Bayesian networks were introduced by Pearl in the 1980s as a formalism for probabilistic reasoning. The causal interpretation (using do‑calculus) emerged in the 1990s, turning them into Causal Bayesian Networks.

chronology.event: Pearl publishes 'Probabilistic Reasoning in Intelligent Systems'
Pearl adds intervention semantics, creating Causal Bayesian Networks

relationships.description: This blueprint is a subtype of the generic causal model blueprint.

name: Granger Causality
description: X causes Y if past values of X improve prediction of Y beyond Y’s own history.

historicalContext.summary: Granger causality was proposed by Clive Granger in 1969 as a statistical notion of predictive causality for time series. It became widely used in econometrics and neuroscience, though it is not a true causal model (only predictive).

chronology.event: Granger publishes 'Investigating Causal Relations by Econometric Models'
Granger receives Nobel Prize for this method

specific.transformationRule: test of predictive improvement via autoregressive models

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.

name: Transfer Entropy
description: Quantifies directional information flow from X to Y.

historicalContext.summary: Transfer entropy was introduced by Thomas Schreiber in 2000 as an information‑theoretic measure of directed dependency, building on Granger causality and earlier work on mutual information.

chronology.event: Schreiber publishes 'Measuring Information Transfer'
Transfer entropy linked to Granger causality for Gaussian variables

specific.transformationRule: conditional mutual information between past of X and future of Y

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.

name: Intervention
description: Manipulating one factor to observe change in another; basis for experiments and do-calculus.

historicalContext.summary: Intervention has been central to experimental science since Bacon. In causal inference, it was formalized by Pearl through the do(·) operator and by Woodward in the manipulationist theory of causation.

chronology.event: Bacon's 'Novum Organum' emphasizes experimental intervention
Woodward publishes 'Making Things Happen'
Pearl formalizes intervention with do(·) operator

relationships.description: This act is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Act.
Intervention acts on causal models.

name: Confounder
description: Influences both cause and effect, potentially biasing causal inference if not controlled for.

historicalContext.summary: The problem of confounding has been recognized since early statistics (Pearson, Fisher). Formal definitions came from epidemiology and graphical models; Pearl characterized confounders via the back‑door criterion.

chronology.event: Yule demonstrates spurious correlation due to confounding
Cornfield discusses confounding in epidemiology
Pearl introduces back‑door criterion for confounder control

relationships.description: This constraint is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Constraint.
Confounders distort causal effect estimates.

name: Counterfactual (statement)
description: A claim about what would happen if some event had or had not occurred; e.g., 'If A had not occurred, B would not have occurred.'

historicalContext.summary: Counterfactual statements have been used in philosophy and science for centuries. In causal inference, they are the target of many queries (e.g., 'What would be the outcome if treatment were different?').

chronology.event: Hume uses counterfactual reasoning
Lewis makes counterfactuals central to his theory of causation
Pearl operationalizes counterfactuals with SCM

relationships.description: This artifact is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Artifact.
Counterfactual statements are expressed in causal models.
Counterfactual statements are produced by counterfactual reasoning.

name: Causal Inference
description: Estimating the effects of interventions from observational or experimental data; includes steps like model specification, identification, estimation, and validation.

historicalContext.summary: Causal inference as a formal discipline grew from statistics (Fisher, Neyman, Rubin) and computer science (Pearl). It is now a standard process in medicine, economics, and AI.

chronology.event: Fisher introduces randomized experiments
Rubin's potential outcomes framework
Pearl's graphical approach
Causal inference becomes standard in data science

relationships.description: This process is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Process.

name: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
description: An experimental design that randomly assigns subjects to treatment/control groups to establish causal relationships.

historicalContext.summary: RCTs originated in agricultural experiments (Fisher) and biomedical research. They are considered the gold standard for establishing causality because randomization eliminates confounding.

chronology.event: Fisher introduces randomization in agricultural trials
First modern RCT: streptomycin trial for tuberculosis
RCTs become mandatory for drug approval

specific.transformationRule: random assignment to treatment/control

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.

name: Causal Discovery
description: Algorithms to learn causal structure (e.g., DAGs) from observational or interventional data.

historicalContext.summary: Causal discovery emerged from the fusion of graphical models and machine learning in the 1990s. Key algorithms include PC, FCI, and GES, later extended with deep learning.

chronology.event: Spirtes, Glymour, and Scheines publish 'Causation, Prediction, and Search'
Pearl's IC algorithm
Chickering presents GES (Greedy Equivalence Search)
Deep learning for causal discovery (e.g., DAG-GNN)

specific.transformationRule: learns graphical structure from conditional independence tests or score-based search

relationships.description: This operator is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Operator.
Causal discovery produces causal models.

name: Against Reification / Layered Causality
description: Causality is not a single mechanism but a bundle of layered processes (biological, social, institutional). Do not treat abstract causal estimates as 'the cause'.

historicalContext.summary: This principle reflects a critique of naive causal inference that conflates statistical estimates with ontological mechanisms. It draws on systems thinking, critical realism, and philosophy of science (Bhaskar, Cartwright).

chronology.event: Bhaskar's 'A Realist Theory of Science' critiques causal reification
Cartwright's 'Nature's Capacities and Their Measurement'
Adoption in complex systems and methodologists

specific.formalStatement: Do not treat causal estimates as the unique underlying mechanism

relationships.description: This principle is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Principle.

name: Reality Segment
description: Some phenomenon or subset of the real world you want to understand (e.g., physical, biological, social system).

historicalContext.summary: The notion of a 'piece of reality' as a target of inquiry is implicit in all empirical science. It is a philosophical primitive: the domain that exists independently of representations.

chronology.event: Aristotle distinguishes substance and accident
Logical positivism's distinction between protocol sentences and reality

relationships.description: This domain is a subtype of the abstract category Epistemic Domain.

name: Barnes–Hut Simulation
description: A computational epistemic process implementing the Barnes–Hut algorithm to simulate large-scale N-body dynamical systems through hierarchical spatial decomposition and approximate force aggregation.
longDescription: Barnes–Hut simulation is a structured epistemic process situated at the intersection of computational physics, numerical simulation, and algorithmic systems modeling. It operationalizes the Barnes–Hut algorithm as an ordered epistemic activity for approximating gravitational or force interactions among large numbers of entities while reducing computational complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n). The process constructs a hierarchical tree-based spatial partition (typically quadtree or octree), recursively decomposes physical space, estimates distant aggregate forces through center-of-mass approximations, and iteratively updates system state trajectories over time. As an epistemic practice, it transforms encoded initial conditions into predictive dynamical artifacts under formal physical laws. It depends on representational blueprints (tree structures, force equations), operators (recursive decomposition, approximation heuristics), constraints (approximation error, timestep instability), and standards (numerical stability, physical plausibility). Barnes–Hut simulation functions as both a scientific modeling pipeline and an algorithmic infrastructure for scalable dynamical knowledge generation.
functionalRoles: Transformation of initial state artifacts into temporal state trajectories

historicalContext.summary: Developed in 1986 by Josh Barnes and Piet Hut to address computational inefficiency in N-body simulations, the Barnes–Hut method became foundational in computational astrophysics and large-scale force simulation.

chronology.event: Barnes and Hut publish hierarchical tree algorithm
Broad adoption in galaxy and cosmological simulations
Extension into GPU and distributed architectures

specific.validationMethod: Numerical convergence analysis, physical conservation checks, approximation error benchmarking

relationships.description: Implements recursive spatial decomposition for computational efficiency.
Uses the Barnes–Hut algorithm as its formal computational operator.
Expresses simulation state and partitioning through hierarchical tree blueprints.
Bounded by timestep stability, force approximation error, and finite precision.
Produces evolving concrete epistemic artifacts representing system trajectories.
Evaluated through conservation laws, error analysis, and simulation robustness.
Depends on computational infrastructure for scalability.

categoryAmbiguity.primaryJustification: Primarily classified as an epistemic process because it is an ordered operational activity transforming inputs into validated predictive artifacts over time.

alternativeCategories.justification: The Barnes–Hut algorithm itself functions as a transformation operator.
Can also function as part of broader computational simulation frameworks.
Specific simulation instances produce fully specified predictive artifacts.

metadata.sourceReference: Conceptual synthesis of Barnes–Hut simulation within epistemic systems ontology

auditTrail.reviewNote: Initial formal epistemic classification and schema encoding completed.

name: Algorithm
description: A formally specified, finite, ordered sequence of operations that transforms defined inputs into outputs according to explicit procedural rules.
longDescription: An algorithm is an abstract procedural structure that defines a reproducible sequence of operations for transforming inputs into outputs under specified logical, mathematical, or computational constraints. Algorithms serve as core epistemic operators by operationalizing inference, optimization, simulation, search, control, and decision procedures into executable form. They may be deterministic, stochastic, recursive, heuristic, exact, or approximate, and can exist independently of specific hardware or implementation environments. Algorithms are foundational to scientific reasoning, engineering systems, mathematics, computational infrastructures, and cognitive procedures because they encode tractable pathways for systematically manipulating representational artifacts. Their significance lies not merely in execution, but in their role as formalized mechanisms for structured transformation within epistemic systems.

historicalContext.summary: Algorithms emerged from early mathematical procedures and were formally generalized through logic, computability theory, and modern computer science.

chronology.event: Euclidean procedural mathematics
Al-Khwarizmi's procedural arithmetic methods
Turing formalizes computation through Turing machines
Modern computational algorithmics expands

specific.transformationRule: Executes a finite sequence of formally defined state transformations from input to output.
specific.operandType: symbolic, numeric, logical, or physical representations
specific.computationalComplexity: Varies by implementation and problem class

relationships.description: Algorithms operationalize formal logical or mathematical principles into executable procedures.
Algorithms are encoded through procedural blueprints such as pseudocode, flowcharts, or programming languages.
Algorithms transform structured epistemic artifacts into new informational states.
Algorithmic performance is constrained by time, space, and tractability limitations.
Algorithms enable broader epistemic processes such as modeling, inference, experimentation, and optimization.

categoryAmbiguity.primaryJustification: Algorithm is primarily an epistemic operator because its defining feature is formal transformation of representations via executable procedural rules.

alternativeCategories.justification: Algorithms may also be treated as abstract representational procedural structures independent of execution.
When instantiated temporally in real systems, algorithms function as operational processes.
Certain algorithms encode adaptive search or optimization strategies.

metadata.sourceReference: Conceptual synthesis from computability theory, computer science, systems theory, and epistemology.

auditTrail.reviewNote: Initial formalization of algorithm as an epistemic operator with category ambiguity analysis.

name: ElectromagneticWave
description: A self-propagating transverse wave of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that travels through vacuum or matter at the speed of light, carrying energy and momentum without requiring a material medium.
longDescription: Electromagnetic waves are solutions to Maxwell's equations in free space or dielectric media, characterized by frequency, wavelength, amplitude, polarization, and phase. They form the basis for all classical electrodynamic radiation, from radio waves to gamma rays. As an ontic phenomenon, the electromagnetic wave is a real, measurable disturbance in the electromagnetic field, existing independently of any particular measurement apparatus. Its behavior is governed by the wave equation derived from Maxwell's equations, and it exhibits wave-particle duality at the quantum level (photons). In classical ontology, it is a perdurant processual entity that propagates causally and can be superposed, reflected, refracted, diffracted, and absorbed. Its observability is direct via antennas, photodetectors, or the human eye (for visible light), though some properties (e.g., phase) require interferometric techniques.

historicalContext.summary: The concept of electromagnetic waves emerged from the unification of electricity and magnetism by James Clerk Maxwell in the 1860s, who predicted their existence. Heinrich Hertz experimentally generated and detected radio waves in 1887, confirming Maxwell's theory. Later developments included the discovery of X-rays by Röntgen (1895), gamma rays by Villard (1900), and the quantum formulation by Einstein (1905) and others, leading to wave-particle duality.

chronology.event: Maxwell publishes 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field', predicting electromagnetic waves.
chronology.associatedContext: Unification of electricity, magnetism, and light; wave equation derived.

chronology.event: Hertz experimentally generates and detects radio waves, confirming Maxwell's prediction.
chronology.associatedContext: First controlled production and measurement of electromagnetic waves in the laboratory.

chronology.event: Einstein explains the photoelectric effect using light quanta (photons), introducing wave-particle duality.
chronology.associatedContext: Quantum nature challenges purely classical wave ontology.

specific.conceptEvaluationNote: The electromagnetic wave is a highly successful ontic concept in classical physics, enabling precise predictions of propagation, interference, and radiation. However, at quantum scales it is supplanted by the photon field description, though classical waves remain a valid approximation for many phenomena (coherent states). As an epistemic tool, it provides an intuitive visualizable model but must be complemented by quantum field theory for completeness.

relationships.description: An electromagnetic wave is composed of oscillating electric and magnetic fields mutually inducing each other.
The behavior of electromagnetic waves is formalized by Maxwell's equations in vacuum or media.
Electromagnetic waves produce radiation pressure and momentum transfer upon absorption or reflection.

categoryAmbiguity.primaryJustification: Electromagnetic wave is a fundamental ontic entity in classical electromagnetism, directly referring to a real physical phenomenon, not an epistemic artifact or process.

metadata.sourceReference: Derived from Maxwell's equations, standard electrodynamics textbooks (e.g., Jackson, Griffiths), and historical analysis.

name: Number
description: An abstract symbolic and structural construct used to represent quantity, magnitude, order, relation, ratio, measure, and formal mathematical structure.
longDescription: A number is an abstract representational construct emerging from the epistemic need to stabilize, compare, manipulate, and formalize quantitative and relational aspects of reality. Numbers function simultaneously as symbolic artifacts, structural invariants, operational operands, and formal entities within mathematical systems. Historically, numbers evolved from concrete counting practices and resource accounting into highly abstract algebraic, topological, logical, and computational objects. Different numerical systems—including natural numbers, integers, rationals, reals, complex numbers, ordinals, cardinals, and hyperreal systems—extend the representational and operational capacity of mathematics. Numbers are foundational to measurement, computation, geometry, modeling, simulation, optimization, finance, physics, engineering, and formal reasoning. Epistemically, numbers mediate between observed phenomena and formal symbolic systems, enabling compression, prediction, abstraction, and operational control over reality models.
functionalRoles: Act as operands in computational systems
Enable abstraction of physical and conceptual systems

historicalContext.summary: The concept of number evolved from primitive counting systems into increasingly abstract mathematical structures used across scientific, computational, and formal domains.

chronology.event: Primitive tally systems emerge
chronology.associatedContext: Early human counting and resource tracking

chronology.event: Babylonian positional numeral systems develop
Greek mathematical abstraction of numbers
Formalization of zero and arithmetic rules in India
Expansion of real and complex number systems
Cantor develops set-theoretic foundations of cardinality
chronology.associatedContext: Infinite set theory and formal mathematics

chronology.event: Formal computational treatment of numbers

specific.formalism: Arithmetic, algebraic, logical, set-theoretic, and computational formal systems
expressiveLimits: Cannot fully represent continuous physical reality with finite precision
Some numerical truths are undecidable within formal systems
Finite computational representations introduce approximation and rounding errors
Symbolic representation may diverge from ontological structure of reality

categoryAmbiguity.primaryJustification: The concept of number is classified primarily as an epistemicBlueprint because it functions as a formal representational structure used to model and manipulate quantitative relationships across domains.

alternativeCategories.justification: Numbers serve operational roles as operands within transformation systems.
Numerals and written numerical symbols exist as concrete symbolic artifacts.
Entire mathematical systems may organize around numerical structures.
Certain philosophical traditions treat numbers as ontologically real entities.

metadata.sourceReference: Synthetic conceptual synthesis derived from mathematics, philosophy of mathematics, logic, computation theory, and epistemology.

auditTrail.reviewNote: Initial conceptual formalization of the number concept using the Conceptual Structure Typing Node Schema.
